s1-fn1: The word !‘jaya’ means victory and was also the title of an original and shorter version of the Mahabharata. So this invocation, which is not part of the main text, can be interpreted in two different ways—a literal uttering of the word ‘victory’ or a recital of the Mahabharata.
s1-fn2: Nara and Narayana were ancient sages, invariably referred to together. But Narayana eventually came to be identiﬁed with Vishnu (hence Krishna) and Nara with Arjuna.
s1-fn3: Head or chief. But in this context, a sage who feeds and teaches 10,000 students.
s1-fn5: A suta was the son of a Kshatriya father and a Brahmana mother and by profession sutas were charioteers. But they were also bards and raconteurs.
s1-fn6: The Puranas are sacred texts, composed by the sage Vyasadeva. There are eighteen major Puranas.
s1-fn8: Vedavyasa or Vyasadeva, thus named because he classiﬁed the Vedas. Vedavyasa or Vyasadeva is a title and there has been more than one sage with such a title. This particular Vedavyasa’s name was Krishna Dvaipayana: Krishna because he was dark in complexion and Dvaipayana because he was born on an island (dvipa).
s1-fn9: Meaning the ﬁrst three castes: Brahmanas, Kshatriyas and Vaishyas. Twice-born (dvija) is also used speciﬁcally for Brahmanas, the second birth referring to the donning of the sacred thread. The ﬁrst three castes were all entitled to this right.
s1-fn10: Sages with knowledge of the supreme being (brahman).
s1-fn11: Sacred texts, four in number: Rig Veda, Sama Veda, Yajur Veda and Atharva Veda.
s1-fn13: Ruler, master, lord. Ishana is a name for Shiva, Vishnu, even the sun. But here, the general meaning is intended.
s1-fn14: The word brahman or paramatman refers to the supreme spirit and should not be confused with Brahmana, though they have the same etymological root.
s1-fn16: Royal sages. Brahmarshis were of the Brahmana caste. Rajarshis were Kshatriyas, but obtained the status of being a sage (rishi) through their learning.
s1-fn17: Union between the human soul and the supreme being.
s1-fn19: Sacred texts in the category of revelation.
s1-fn20: Collections of sacred texts, particularly in verse form, speciﬁcally the Vedas.
s1-fn24: Krishna’s name, as the son of Vasudeva.
s1-fn25: These are two (Asita and Devala) ancient sages, whose names always occur together.
s1-fn26: A semi-divine species, singers and musicians of the gods.
s1-fn31: Those in the celibate stage of life.
s1-fn35: A great sacriﬁce, at the time of coronation, when other kings and princes pay tribute and accept the superiority of the crowned universal monarch.
s1-fn40: A Brahmana who has ﬁnished his period of study and celibacy (brahmacharya) and is about to enter (or just entered) the householder stage (garhasthya) is known as a snataka.
s1-fn45: A large army with 21,870 chariots, 21,870 elephants, 65,610 horses and 109,350 foot soldiers.
s1-fn46: This is a reference to the ﬁfth incarnation of Vishnu, when in the form of a vamana (dwarf), he had humbled the demon king Bali.
s1-fn50: Instead of tens of thousands, this can also be translated as many.
s1-fn54: There was a hierarchy of warriors. Maharatha can loosely be translated as great warrior. However, more speciﬁcally, a maharatha was a warrior who could take on ten thousand warriors single-handed.
s1-fn55: Warriors who had taken an oath never to withdraw from the ﬁeld of battle.
s1-fn65: Indra was the king of the gods. The celestial spear (shakti) was given by Indra to Karna, though Karna is not mentioned by name in this shloka. The next shloka is explicit.
s1-fn73: That is, circuits of the club.
s1-fn75: Droupadi was Drupada’s daughter and Drupada was the king of Panchala.
s1-fn82: Earth, water, energy, wind and sky.
s1-fn84: Literally, history. More speciﬁcally, the two epics.
s1-fn85: Though sacred texts, the Vedas have been personiﬁed here.
s1-fn86: Reference here is to Krishna Dvaipayana (Vedavyasa).
s2-fn1: A very holy region, identiﬁed with the area around Kurukshetra.
s2-fn2: Two of the four yugas or eras, the four being satya (krita), treta, dvapara and kali.
s2-fn8: The supreme soul, the brahman or the paramatman.
s2-fn9: These numbers are not part of the text. They have been added so that the reader can get a better sense of the one hundred books.
s2-fn10: The burning of the house of lac.
s2-fn27: The sequel to the game of dice.
s2-fn31: The travel to the world of Indra.
s2-fn35: Named after a sage known as Markandeya.
s2-fn41: Arani is a wood or stick used for kindling a sacred ﬁre and this parva is named after that.
s2-fn42: Virata was the king of the Matsya kingdom and the Pandavas lived there in exile.
s2-fn48: An arrival in search of peace.
s2-fn51: One of the seven continents (dvipa) that surround Mount Meru. Sometimes, the number of continents is also given as eighteen. But seven is more standard. The word dvipa also means island. But here, it has been used in the alternative meaning, a division of the terrestrial world.
s2-fn59: The word souptika means something connected with sleep and the name is a reference to the fact that the protagonists were asleep and the incidents took place at night.
s2-fn69: Concerning the future. The Harivamsha is usually accepted as an appendix to the Mahabharata, but not what is referred to as Bhavishya parva. Bhavishya parva is what is known as Bhavishya Purana, one of the eighteen major Puranas. Barring this reference, Bhavishya parva is not part of the Mahabharata. Consequently, it doesn’t ﬁnd a mention in Section 1’s listing either.
s2-fn70: Indra’s horse, which emerged as a result of the churning of the ocean.
s2-fn71: This is the sixth parva, listed as the descent of the ﬁrst generation. After this, one loses the matching between the eighteen-parva classiﬁcation and the 100-parva classiﬁcation, since the text now sticks to the eighteen-parva classiﬁcation.
s2-fn72: One may loosely use the word demon for both daitya and danava, but daityas are descendants of Diti and danavas are descendants of Danu.
s2-fn73: Nagas are not snakes. Snakes are sarpas. Nagas can perhaps be translated as serpents. Unlike snakes, nagas can assume other forms (such as human) and live in separate geographical regions, not earth. They are also sometimes semi-divine. Sarpas live on earth.
s2-fn79: Jarasandha was the king of Magadha and he had imprisoned the kings in his capital, Girivraja.
s2-fn82: The ﬂower of the coral tree, one of the ﬁve trees in heaven.
s2-fn83: Kartyavirya was the king of the Haihayas. This Rama is Parashurama, son of Jamadagni. Kartyavirya was killed by Parashurama because he abducted Jamadagni’s sacred cow.
s2-fn90: A large tree that was believed to contain ﬁre inside it.
s2-fn97: Arjuna’s name is Kiriti, meaning the diademed one.
s2-fn102: The critical edition has sixty-four chapters.
s2-fn103: Pritha is Kunti’s name and the sons of Pritha are the Pandavas.
s2-fn106: Dronacharya was sage Bharadvaja’s son and was the preceptor, while Ashvatthama was Dronacharya’s son. Because he was Bharadvaja’s son, Dronacharya was known as Bhaaradvaja. The absence of diacritical marks makes distinction between Bharadvaja (the father) and Bhaaradvaja (the son) difﬁcult, especially when we write Bhaaradvaja as Bharadvaja to make the translation smooth. However, the context should make it clear whether Bharadvaja or Bhaaradvaja is meant.
s2-fn107: Arjuna. Phalguni and Phalguna are used synonymously.
s2-fn109: Bhishma was on a bed of arrows when he told this.
s2-fn115: The critical edition has ninety-six chapters.
s2-fn118: The critical edition has forty-seven chapters.
s2-fn120: The men of the Vrishni lineage.
s2-fn124: Krishna’s father. Vasudeva is Krishna’s father and therefore, Krishna is known as Vaasudeva. The absence of diacritical marks makes distinction between Vasudeva and Vaasudeva difﬁcult, especially because Vaasudeva is written as Vasudeva to make the translation smooth. However, the context should make it clear whether Vasudeva (the father) or Vaasudeva (the son) is meant.
s2-fn130: Earth, water, energy, wind and sky.
s2-fn131: Those born alive from wombs, those born from eggs, those that are plants and those that are born from sweat (insects).
s2-fn132: Purana means ancient account or tale and there are eighteen great Puranas, each describing creation, destruction, different eras, genealogies and the histories of the solar and lunar dynasties.
s3-fn1: The dog is referred to as sarameya, meaning progeny of Sarama. Sarama was the dog of the gods and the ancestor of all dogs.
s3-fn4: The sun-plant, more like a bush.
s3-fn5: The twin physicians of the gods. They were sons of the sun.
s3-fn9: The nave of the wheel is the year and the sum of 720 is obtained by adding 360 days and 360 nights.
s3-fn10: The meaning is not clear and the number twenty probably represents the gap between solar days and lunar days.
s3-fn11: Twelve months or twelve signs of the zodiac.
s3-fn13: The ten cardinal points, including zenith (above) and nadir (below).
s3-fn17: That is, the preceptor must receive payment (dakshina) after the successful completion of training and the disciple must give it.
s3-fn20: By saying that it was unclean.
s3-fn21: The word used is kshapanaka, which means not just any mendicant, but a Buddhist or a Jain one.
s3-fn22: The word used now is sarpa, not naga. That is, these two words are being used synonymously here.
s3-fn23: Airavata is Indra’s elephant. But this Airavata is a king of the nagas.
s3-fn24: This is a different Dhritarashtra; another name for Airavata.
s3-fn25: Takshaka’s son. Arjuna killed Ashvasena’s mother. In his search for revenge, Ashvasena entered Karna’s weapon in an attempt to kill Arjuna, an attempt that failed.
s3-fn26: Not to be confused with Janamejaya’s brother, who had the same name.
s3-fn27: All these are salutations to Indra.
s3-fn30: This is Indra’s horse Ucchaihshrava, which emerged from the churning of the ocean.
s3-fn33: The god of rain, also Indra.
s3-fn35: Takshaka killed Janamejaya’s father Parikshit. That was the reason Janamejaya undertook a sacriﬁce to destroy all serpents.
s3-fn36: The sage Kashyapa, who tried to restrain Takshaka from killing Parikshit.
s4-fn1: The numbering of chapters is sequential throughout the translation, instead of separate numbering of chapters within sections. This is also the pattern followed in the critical edition.
s4-fn5: The word means to be deprived of, or perishing.
s4-fn7: Maintenance of the special ﬁre (Agni) and the offering of oblations to it.
s4-fn9: Sacriﬁces on the day of the new moon.
s4-fn10: Sacriﬁces on the day of the full moon.
s4-fn11: There were originally thirty-three gods—eleven in heaven, eleven in the sky and eleven on earth. Another way of getting the number is by adding twelve adityas, eleven rudras and ten vishvadevas.
s4-fn12: Exclamations made at the time of offering oblations.
s4-fn14: Exclamations made at the time of offering oblations.
s4-fn16: An apsara. Apsaras are celestial maidens associated with Indra’s court. They are sometimes regarded as wives of gandharvas.
s4-fn22: A snake that is not poisonous.
s5-fn1: The word prajapati means lord of the world and has different nuances. It is a name for Brahma. However, those who were appointed by Brahma to rule over the worlds are also known as Prajapati. Accordingly, Daksha is also known as Prajapati and the reference here is to Daksha.
s5-fn2: The word used is yayavaras, an expression also used by Jaratkaru’s ancestors.
s5-fn3: Vasuki is a great naga or serpent and is the king of the nagas. Vasuki was the rope when the ocean was churned by the gods and the demons.
s5-fn4: Kadru and Vinata were daughters of Daksha and were married to the sage Kashyapa. Kadru was the mother of the snakes and as the story unfolds, we will learn that Kadru cursed her offspring because they did not side with her in her rivalry with Vinata.
s5-fn10: The son. Aruna is the red sky of dawn and is also the sun’s charioteer.
s5-fn13: Mythical beings with human bodies and heads of horses.
s5-fn14: A yojana is a measure of distance and is equal to four kroshas. A yojana is between 13 and 15 kilometres.
s5-fn15: Ananta literally means without end and is a name for the naga Shesha, on whom Vishnu rests, and who is Vasuki’s elder brother. Instructed by Vishnu or Narayana, Ananta uprooted Mandara.
s5-fn17: In a loose sense, asuras and danavas are both demons. But asura is more of a general term and means the antithesis of suras or gods. Danavas are more speciﬁc demons, in the sense that they are the offspring of Danu. Daityas are also speciﬁc demons, in the sense that they are the offspring of Diti.
s5-fn27: The heads were adorned with gold ornaments.
s5-fn28: Weapons had to be summoned with the power of the mind.
s5-fn32: Indra. Indra had killed a demon named Bala.
s5-fn33: Someone wearing a crown or diadem. Kiriti is another name for Arjuna. In this context, it probably means the god Nara.
s5-fn35: This should not cause confusion. The word Kourava is often used as opposite to Pandava. The Pandavas had Pandu as their father. However, Kuru was a common ancestor to both brothers. Hence any Pandava, and anyone descended from the Pandavas, can also be called Kourava.
s5-fn38: The word used is timingila. A whale is a timi, a timingila is that which swallows whales.
s5-fn39: The word used is makara. Since a makara is a mythical being, it is impossible to translate it satisfactorily. But shark or crocodile is close enough.
s5-fn41: Vishnu. This is a reference to Vishnu’s boar (varaha) incarnation.
s5-fn43: As will be evident, this really means the gods.
s5-fn45: Another name for Garuda, meaning the one with beautiful feathers.
s5-fn48: The word means destroyer of cities.
s5-fn53: A kshana is a small interval of time, the time it takes for a twinkling of the eye and is equal to four-ﬁfths of a second. A lava is even smaller, because it is one-sixth of a kshana.
s5-fn54: The bright half of the lunar month.
s5-fn55: The dark half of the lunar month. The term used in the text is bahula.
s5-fn56: The daily increase or decrease in the size of the moon. A kala is the span of a zodiac sign divided by 1800. So it is roughly around 1 minute, as in minutes and degrees. Measured in time, a kala is variously deﬁned as 1 minute, 48 seconds or 8 seconds.
s5-fn58: Very small interval of time, equal to half of a lava.
s5-fn61: Makara. However, a makara is actually a mythical being.
s5-fn64: The Malaya mountains are in south India, adjoining Malabar. The Malaya mountains had several sandalwood trees. Thus, an alternative translation is that the island had many sandalwood trees, since malaya also means a sandalwood tree.
s5-fn67: Amrita is nectar or ambrosia and was produced when the ocean was churned.
s5-fn68: Hunters, ﬁshermen. Usually, they are described as dwelling in the mountains.
s5-fn70: Supratika is a common name for an elephant and this elephant should not be confused with Bhagadatta’s elephant Supratika.
s5-fn71: A yojana is between 13 and 15 kilometres.
s5-fn75: Rishis who number 60,000 and were generated from the creator’s body. They are the size of a thumb and precede the sun’s chariot.
s5-fn80: The performer of a hundred sacriﬁces, Indra.
s5-fn81: Great sage and preceptor of the gods.
s5-fn85: A tree with large, fragrant and orange-coloured ﬂowers, the Butea frondosa.
s5-fn94: Aruna is the dawn and also the sun’s charioteer.
s5-fn95: Vishvakarma is the architect of the gods and Bhouvana (or Bhoumana) is one of his names.
s5-fn100: That is, enter the place where the amrita was.
s5-fn105: Dhritarashtra is also the name of a naga and this should not be confused with the Dhritarashtra who was Duryodhana’s father.
s5-fn108: This can also be translated as the forest of Pushkara.
s5-fn114: These words are being addressed to Vasuki.
s5-fn123: Vaivasvata is one of Yama’s names.
s5-fn124: This refers to Shamika’s message, not the curse. The curse was levied by Shamika’s son, Shringi.
s5-fn127: The name used in the text is Nagasahnya.
s5-fn129: Yudhishthira, who in a sense was also the great-grandfather, though not as directly as Arjuna.
s5-fn131: An ancient king of the lunar dynasty and therefore an ancestor of the Kouravas and the Pandavas. Pururava married the apsara Urvashi.
s5-fn132: That is, the merit obtained through these austerities.
s5-fn133: A brahmachari, usually translated as celibate. However, a brahmachari is strictly someone who treads the path of the brahman.
s5-fn135: Shvetakakiya. This is an unhappy translation, though white crow is right. However, the sense is that the dedication has the watchfulness of a dog, the timidity of a deer and the instinct of a crow.
s5-fn137: Agnihotra is a sacriﬁcial ﬁre. The text means that the time for making offerings to the sacriﬁcial ﬁre has arrived.
s5-fn138: Asti can be translated as ‘it is there’. Jaratkaru meant that an embryo was there in the womb.
s5-fn139: Shiva, the one with a trident in his hand.
s5-fn140: Dhanur Veda. This is about the science of ﬁghting and weaponry.
s5-fn142: Soubhadra means Subhadra’s son, that is, Abhimanyu. Parikshit was Abhimanyu’s son. Parikshina means to become weak, thin, lean, emaciated. Since this is what had happened to the Kuru lineage, he came to be known as Parikshit.
s5-fn143: Kama (desire), krodha (anger), lobha (greed), mada (ego), moha (delusion) and matsarya (envy).
s5-fn145: Takshaka had assumed the disguise of a Brahmana.
s5-fn146: This is now a reference to Kashyapa.
s5-fn148: Superintending or assisting priests; probably means assisting here.
s5-fn149: Ofﬁciating priests. Usually, there were four types of ritvijas—hotar (one who recited prayers, identiﬁed with Rig Veda), udgatar (one who chanted or sang prayers, identiﬁed with Sama Veda), adhvaryu (one who ofﬁciated, identiﬁed with Yajur Veda) and brahman (chief priest, identiﬁed with Atharva Veda).
s5-fn153: Send the sacriﬁce up to the gods.
s5-fn159: The text should literally be translated as the staff of a Brahmana.
s5-fn164: The ﬁre. Vibhavasu is also a name for the sun.
s5-fn165: The ﬁre. Chitrabhanu is also a name for the sun.
s6-fn1: Satyavati, who was dark in colour.
s6-fn3: Shantanu was Bhishma’s father and also the father of Chitrangada and Vichitravirya. The Kouravas and the Pandavas were descended from Vichitravirya.
s6-fn10: The word in the original is pitamaha, which means paternal grandfather, as well as being a name for Brahma. But it is also generically used for all ancestors.
s6-fn21: Literally, winner of riches and one of Arjuna’s names.
s6-fn25: The forest Khandava. The story about why Agni was allowed to burn Khandava forest will be told later. The ﬁre-god had been suffering from indigestion and this was a cure.
s6-fn30: Ayuta, meaning many in general, and ten thousand in particular.
s6-fn34: This can also speciﬁcally be translated as Himalaya.
s6-fn35: Days of the full moon and days of the new moon.
s6-fn36: That is, is freed from the desire of attaining heaven.
s6-fn45: Maharatha can loosely be translated as great warrior. However, more speciﬁcally, a maharatha was a warrior who could take on 10,000 warriors single-handed.
s6-fn47: Literally, someone who travels through the upper regions.
s6-fn48: The mountain lusted for the river.
s6-fn58: Dharma has four legs in Satya Yuga, three in Treta Yuga, two in Dvapara Yuga and one in Kali Yuga. The word leg (pada) is used metaphorically and the word also means one quarter. Thus, completion has four quarters, which is what one has in Satya Yuga. Therefore, it becomes 75% in Treta Yuga, 50% in Dvapara Yuga and 25% in Kali Yuga.
s6-fn59: The word vyasa means to distribute or divide.
s6-fn60: Yama, the god of death and justice.
s6-fn61: Shakuntika in the original text. Shakuntika also means bird, but given the context, locust or cricket seems more appropriate.
s6-fn63: Sanjaya was a suta, that is, a charioteer-cum-raconteur. He was an aide to Dhritarashtra, who was blind, and Sanjaya has become famous because he was granted divine sight so that he could describe the incidents occurring in the Kurukshetra war to Dhritarashtra from a distance. Sanjaya’s father was Gavalgana.
s6-fn64: Kavacha in the original text. A charm that works like armour is another possible translation.
s6-fn66: Son of Sharadvata, also known as Kripacharya.
s6-fn69: Subala was the king of Gandhara.
s6-fn70: Gandhari, Subala’s daughter and Shakuni’s sister.
s6-fn75: This Shrutasena should not be confused with Janamejaya’s brother.
s6-fn76: The king of Panchala and the father of Dhrishtadyumna and Droupadi.
s6-fn79: This is a reference to Shesha naga.
s7-fn3: One should not expect consistency in accounts of creation. In some accounts, Marichi was born from Brahma and Marichi’s son was Kashyapa, from whom all humans are descended through Manu. In other accounts, Brahma had seven sons born from his mental powers, with Vashishtha added to the list of six that is given in the text. These seven (or six) are prajapatis or rulers of the world. In other accounts, ten prajapatis were born from Brahma’s right thumb and one of these was Daksha. Daksha had several daughters, thirteen were married to Kashyapa and twentyseven were married to the moon or Chandra. Section 1 states that Daksha had seven sons and that Daksha was born from an ancient sage named Prachetas. But this is a rare reference. Daksha’s birth from Brahma is more common.
s7-fn4: The world was being populated by offspring of the gods, and the demons are also descended from Daksha’s daughters. In fact, every living species is descended from Daksha’s daughters.
s7-fn8: Tarkshya is also a name for both Garuda and Garuda’s elder brother Aruna. The text suggests that Garuda and Aruna had another brother named Tarkshya.
s7-fn11: There is an inconsistency with what the text has just said about Prava’s progeny, the gandharvas and the apsaras.
s7-fn13: Mythical beings with human bodies and heads of horses.
s7-fn14: This is a little confusing, because there doesn’t seem to be any difference between kinnaras and kimpurushas.
s7-fn15: These were the valakhilya sages, 60,000 in number. Generated from the creator’s body, they are the size of a thumb and precede the sun’s chariot.
s7-fn17: Daksha. Prajapati is also one of Brahma’s names.
s7-fn18: The daughters effectively become sons in one of two ways. Either, the daughter, since she is regarded as a son, returns to her father’s house after marriage, or the daughter’s son is adopted as a son by his maternal grandfather.
s7-fn25: The word son doesn’t always mean son in a literal sense. It can also mean progeny.
s7-fn29: There are twenty-seven lunar mansions (nakshatras) and krittika is the third, identiﬁed with the Pleiades. However, there are actually six stars in the Pleiades. Hence the use of the plural krittikas, and the krittikas reared Kumara, which is why he is known as Kartikeya.
s7-fn31: Famous sage, preceptor of the gods.
s7-fn32: Vivasvat’s son. Identiﬁed with Vishvakarma, architect of the gods.
s7-fn38: A class of demi-gods; a semi-divine species, Kubera’s attendants.
s7-fn41: That is, set her free from her pregnancy.
s7-fn43: Ourva is another name for Chyavana. He was thus named because he was born through splitting open the thigh (uru).
s7-fn45: Not to be confused with Richika’s son Jamadagni. Richika’s son is the more famous Jamadagni.
s7-fn52: Monkeys with tails like those of cows, with dark bodies and red cheeks.
s7-fn53: The cardinal points are guarded by elephants.
s7-fn55: There were probably two Surasas, one who was Kadru’s daughter and another who was Krodha’s daughter.
s7-fn56: A shyena is a hawk or falcon, Shyeni is the feminine. So Shyeni could have been married to Vinata’s son Aruna, though the text doesn’t make it clear if the two Arunas were the same.
s7-fn63: Literally, those who are slaves to anger.
s7-fn64: This is known as Dhanur Veda.
s7-fn69: The eight vasus, sons of Manu, were reborn on earth as Shantanu’s sons.
s7-fn73: A class of gods often described as ten in number. Their mother was Vishva.
s7-fn78: Barbarians, non-Aryans, those who did not speak Sanskrit.
s7-fn79: The god of rain. That is, Indra.
s7-fn82: That is, Garuda. The speed was like that of Garuda.
s7-fn84: The word used in the text is shatapada, one with six legs. This is a word for a bee.
s7-fn88: A yati is speciﬁcally an ascetic.
s7-fn89: The word used in the text is kaccha. This is usually the word used for a marsh. But a marsh does not seem right, given the context.
s7-fn92: This is probably a reference to the Yajur Veda. The critical edition jumps from the Rig Veda to the Atharva Veda, at least by name. The Yajur Veda is not explicitly mentioned. Some regional variations mention the Yajur Veda explicitly and there are shlokas, missing in the critical edition, that mention the Sama Veda.
s7-fn101: A nakshatra, that is, a star or constellation.
s7-fn102: The wind-god. Vayu is another one of his names.
s7-fn103: The god of love. Kama is another one of his names.
s7-fn104: She is actually repeating Kanva’s words.
s7-fn106: The word used is shakuna. In a general sense, this means bird. But in a speciﬁc sense, it means vultures or kites.
s7-fn108: One of the eight forms of marriage. In this form, there are no ceremonies and no relatives are present. The bride and the groom willingly marry each other in what is a marriage of love.
s7-fn110: It is not clear what this number ﬁve refers to, probably the last ﬁve of the eight mentioned forms.
s7-fn111: With infantry, cavalry, elephants and chariots.
s7-fn113: A wheel, the sign of a king.
s7-fn114: Sarva is everything and damana is to subjugate.
s7-fn115: The word used is yuvaraja, which can be translated as prince or young king. However, this appellation also signiﬁes the person becoming an heir apparent.
s7-fn117: Literally, the city of the elephant and another name for Hastinapura, Duhshanta’s capital.
s7-fn121: There are three kinds of sacriﬁcial ﬁres—ahavaniya, garhapatya and dakshina. The garhapatya ﬁre burns in the household, the ahavaniya ﬁre is the one into which sacriﬁcial offerings are placed and the dakshina ﬁre is the one that faces the south.
s7-fn122: There were originally thirty or thirty-three gods —eleven in heaven, eleven in the sky and eleven on earth. Another way of getting the number is by adding twelve adityas, eleven rudras and ten vishvadevas.
s7-fn124: That is, the gift of a pond.
s7-fn126: A universal monarch or sovereign of the world. Sarvabhouma has the same meaning.
s7-fn127: It is not clear what this means. A padma is a lotus. But this doesn’t quite ﬁt the requirement. Were one thousand lotuses given? A padma is also one trillion. In that case, one trillion of what is left unsaid. Perhaps a padma simply connotes a large number and a large number of cows were given.
s7-fn129: One of six schools of philosophy, attributed to the sage Kapila.
s7-fn130: The word for men is manava.
s7-fn131: Manu is not the name of a single person. There were fourteen Manus, each presiding over the interval between a round of destruction and recreation. The ﬁrst Manu (Svayambhuva) produced the ﬁrst round of human beings. However, the present round of human beings is descended from the seventh Manu (Vaivasvata).
s7-fn133: Robbers or dacoits. The word also means evil beings or demons that were enemies of gods and men. It can also mean outcasts or outcastes.
s7-fn136: Brihaspati, the preceptor of the gods.
s7-fn137: The demons. That is, the demons also had a priest for the same purpose.
s7-fn138: Ushanas is another name for Shukra or Shukracharya, the preceptor of the demons. The text has the word kavya, which has been translated as wise. However, Kavya is also another name for Shukra and Kavya is the son of Kavi.
s7-fn139: The knowledge of bringing the dead back to life.
s7-fn141: Sacriﬁcial ﬁre kindled in the evening. Presumably, this was Kacha’s task.
s7-fn143: The brighter half of the lunar month.
s7-fn146: Indra. Literally, the performer of a hundred sacriﬁces.
s7-fn147: Indra. Literally, the destroyer of cities.
s7-fn149: Meaning that the forest or the wood was beautiful. Chitraratha was the king of the gandharvas.
s7-fn150: The text actually has Devayani addressing Sharmishtha as her disciple. This is because Devayani’s father (Shukra) was the preceptor to Sharmishtha’s father (Vrishaparva).
s7-fn151: One of Devayani’s companions or maids.
s7-fn152: Before the quarrel, Devayani and Sharmishtha were friends.
s7-fn153: There is a break in continuity in the critical edition. Other editions have a shloka where Shukra goes and tells Devayani what has occurred.
s7-fn154: That of giving birth to a child.
s7-fn157: This isn’t clear at all. There was a kingdom of Bhoja in the Vidarbha or Malwa region. But why should that be a curse? Or is this appellation a reference to people who were addicted to food (bhojana)?
s7-fn158: Usually there is reference to three sacriﬁcial ﬁres—ahavaniya, garhapatya and dakshina. The other two sacred ﬁres are samya and avasadhya.
s7-fn160: Clearly, not material prosperity, but riches obtained through righteousness.
s7-fn161: The text should literally be translated as those born from sweat.
s7-fn164: From an earlier reference, one knows that Ashtaka was a king. Yayati was his maternal grandfather.
s7-fn165: The ﬁrst of the four yugas, also known as Satya Yuga.
s7-fn166: The word used is shitikantha, which means white in the neck. This means a peacock, though some kind of vulture with a white neck is also possible.
s7-fn167: That is, through the merits of one’s earlier deeds.
s7-fn168: As enumerated in what immediately follows, the ﬁve sense organs and mind.
s7-fn170: None of this is to be interpreted in a literal sense. For instance, the teeth are clean because he lives on pure food, the nails are clipped because he does not hurt anyone, he is always bathed and without dirt because his ac-
s7-fn171: Pain and pleasure, happiness and unhappiness, and so on.
s7-fn172: Cattle and other animals eat to survive, without making eating the end object.
s7-fn173: The ascetic and the householder who has learning.
s7-fn174: As mentioned earlier, Pratardana was also a king.
s7-fn176: An arbuda is one hundred million.
s7-fn178: Earth, water, energy, air and the sky.
s7-fn179: The suggestion is that the son was actually Bharadvaja’s.
s7-fn180: Fourfold armies include elephants, horses, chariots and infantry.
s7-fn183: The word used is vishana, which means both horn (as in trumpet) and tusk.
s7-fn186: Not to be confused with Parikshit, Arjuna’s grandson.
s7-fn187: Not to be confused with the horse.
s7-fn188: Designed to ensure conquest (jita) of the world (vishva).
s7-fn189: The word prach means eastern or easterly.
s7-fn191: The word used is bhastra, which is a leather bag or pouch, usually used for storing water.
s7-fn193: The word shanta means to appease, calm, satisy, pacify, cure or alleviate.
s7-fn196: Ceremony where the bride chooses her own groom.
s7-fn197: Shulka is price, but this not a marriage where a bride price is paid. Virya means valour, so this is a marriage where the bride is married to the person who exhibits the most valour.
s7-fn199: The word parikshina means diminished, decayed, thin. In this context, even extinct can be used.
s7-fn200: There doesn’t seem to be much point in telling Janamejaya this. Perhaps the intention is to make the account complete.
s7-fn201: A sacriﬁce where offerings of food and water were made.
s7-fn202: Celestial, terrestrial and subterranean. The river Ganga ﬂows in all three regions.
s7-fn203: As mentioned earlier, the word shanta means to appease, calm, satisy, pacify, cure or alleviate. With the same etymological root, earlier, a slightly different twist has been given to the name Shantanu, concerning Shantanu’s traits and not those of his father.
s7-fn207: Ganga. Jahnavi is Ganga’s name because Ganga is Jahnu’s daughter.
s7-fn212: Indra. Literally, one who sacks cities.
s7-fn217: The maintenance of the sacred ﬁre.
s7-fn218: The word three here accordingly refers to the ﬁrst three Vedas.
s7-fn220: Indra, the performer of a hundred sacriﬁces.
s7-fn223: The terrible or fearsome one, because of the vow he took. The word bhishma means terrible or fearssome.
s7-fn224: The gandharva was also named Chitrangada.
s7-fn226: That is, through a system of bride price being paid.
s7-fn227: The eight forms of marriage actually are brahma, daiva, arsha, prajapatya, asura, gandharva, rakshasa and paishacha.
s7-fn231: Soubha is the name of the kingdom. The king’s name is Shalva.
s7-fn235: The king of the Haihayas was Kartavirya Arjuna.
s7-fn236: That is, the person who married the mother, not the one who begot the son.
s7-fn237: The critical edition has eliminated shlokas that tell us Dirghatama was born blind. Hence, the long period of darkness is left unexplained.
s7-fn238: Dirghatama. One now knows that Dirghatama was blind.
s7-fn239: This bull among men is Janamejaya. Although Bhishma is now telling the story to Satyavati, it is being retold by Vaishampayana. However, this reference to Janamejaya shouldn’t have been here, since it is Bhishma who is speaking.
s7-fn241: The word vyasa means to divide or distribute.
s7-fn242: Satyavati’s name. She was so called because she was dark.
s7-fn243: Both Ambika and Ambalika, who were princesses from the kingdom of Kashi. Kousalya means a princess of Kosala.
s7-fn245: The word pandu means pale or white.
s7-fn247: The word ani means point, usually of a needle.
s7-fn248: Animandavya was a child when he pierced the insect.
s7-fn249: Krita Yuga or Satya Yuga, the ﬁrst of the four yugas and a time when dharma was at its best. The incidents of the Mahabharata occurred during Dvapara Yuga, the third of the yugas.
s7-fn251: The word used is kripana. It can also be translated as miserly.
s7-fn253: Because he was born of a Shudra woman.
s7-fn255: Bhishma was speaking to Vidura in particular.
s7-fn257: This is a reference to an incident when the gods refused to give Shiva (Hara) a share in the sacriﬁcial offerings. Shiva ripped out the god Bhaga’s eyes.
s7-fn259: She would face a difﬁculty in having sons.
s7-fn262: Once she was reared by Kuntibhoja, Pritha obtained the name Kunti.
s7-fn264: Vasu means riches and the riches he was born with were the natural armour and earrings.
s7-fn265: Indra. Indra was attempting to protect and help Arjuna.
s7-fn266: A shakti is a spear or lance. But this one was divine.
s7-fn267: Vikartana is the sun, so Vaikartana means the sun’s son. Karna means the ear.
s7-fn268: Indra is Maghavan. His wife is Shachi. Since Shachi was Puloma’s daughter, she is Poulomi.
s7-fn270: Hastinapura. Nagapura is another name for Hastinapura, the city of the elephant. The word naga means elephant.
s7-fn272: There are two Kousalyas, Ambika and Ambalika. This one is Ambalika.
s7-fn273: In this case, father means Bhishma.
s7-fn276: Jayanta is the son of Indra and Shachi.
s7-fn277: Purandara is Indra and Indra’s elephant is Airavata.
s7-fn279: The word used is parashava. This means offspring born of a Brahmana father and a Shudra mother. While the word also means bastard, it couldn’t have been used in that sense. Because in that sense of bastard or mixed breed, Dhritarashtra and Pandu were no different.
s7-fn280: There is a problem with the numbers, since there were only 100 pots.
s7-fn281: This is a reference to Section 6, Adi-vamshavatarana Parva.
s7-fn282: Meaning that the enemy was unprepared.
s7-fn284: That is, a different tree for every night.
s7-fn285: Not beg from more than seven families.
s7-fn286: A name for Vidura, a man born from a Kshatriya woman and a Shudra man.
s7-fn287: The four ashramas—brahmacharya, garhasthya, vanaprastha and sannyasa.
s7-fn288: There is a problem of translation here. What has been translated as superior residents should literally be translated as residents of families. And inferior residents should literally be translated as residents of villages. This doesn’t quite seem right. Hence village is probably being used in an inferior and normative sense.
s7-fn293: These are both heirs and relatives.
s7-fn294: These are neither heirs, nor relatives. As listed, the number adds up to seven and not six. Nor are the differences very clear. For instance, purchased/bought or gifted/presented occur in both categories. In the ﬁrst list, the word for purchased is parikrita. In the second list, the word for purchased is krita. The text doesn’t enable us to deduce the difference and is this also the case with gifted/presented.
s7-fn303: Agni, or ﬁre. Speciﬁcally havya is sacriﬁcial offerings and vahana is the one who bears. Havyavahana is therefore the sacriﬁcial ﬁre into which offerings are placed.
s7-fn306: Bibhatsu is one of Arjuna’s names and means the terrible one.
s7-fn307: Meaning that she was superior in birth to Kunti.
s7-fn308: By obtaining more sons than Kunti.
s7-fn310: That is, he did not succumb to desire with Kunti.
s7-fn312: There were parts of the bodies that had not been burnt. Having said this, there is some contradiction about when the bodies were burnt.
s7-fn313: The word used is vardhaman, which means increasing. This has been translated as the chief gate. But it is perfectly possible for this to have been the proper name for the gate.
s7-fn318: This is yet another name for Hastinapura. The word varana means elephant.
s7-fn321: Kunti’s son. In this case, Bhima.
s7-fn324: The word used is sarathi, which usually means charioteer, but also means companion. Charioteer doesn’t make any sense here, companion makes more sense. The companions are Bhima’s brothers.
s7-fn325: Vidura. It could also mean Yuyutsu.
s7-fn326: There is a break of continuity in the critical edition and Kripa appears out of the blue. In other editions, we are told that Dhritarashtra wanted to ﬁnd a teacher for the princes, since they were getting out of control. Kripa, descended from Goutama, was chosen to teach the art of ﬁghting.
s7-fn327: The word shara means arrow and Sharadvat is one with arrows.
s7-fn328: The science or knowledge of weapons.
s7-fn329: The word kripa means compassion. One of the twins was a boy and he was named Kripa. The other one was a girl and she was named Kripi.
s7-fn331: There are four upavedas or minor vedas, of which, dhanurveda is one. Each upaveda is attached to one of the main vedas. The other three upavedas are ayurveda (medicine), gandharvaveda (music) and sthapatyaveda (architecture).
s7-fn332: There is a break in continuity in the critical edition. Bharadvaja had gone to the Ganga for a bath before making the offerings into the ﬁre.
s7-fn333: Drona. This is usually a wooden vessel.
s7-fn335: Ashva means horse and sthaman means strength or stamina.
s7-fn339: The word used is shrotriya, which can also be translated as a learned Brahmana.
s7-fn342: The word used is vita. I have translated this as ball. But there is no evidence to suggest that this was spherical. In all probability, this was a short piece of wood struck with another long piece of wood, something like a bat. Guli-danda may be the closest approximation.
s7-fn343: The word used is ishika and means reed. In recounting this particular anecdote, renderings invariably interpret this as reed or grass. However, ishika also means arrow and Drona could have used arrows instead of reeds. There is nothing in the text to counter the arrow interpretation.
s7-fn345: Implying weapons that were held in the hand.
s7-fn346: Hunter or ﬁsherman. Usually, described as dwelling in the mountains and forests.
s7-fn347: The text uses the word shabdabheda, which is the art of shooting with the aid of sound alone, without seeing the object. Earlier, there was no suggestion that Ekalavya had not seen the dog when he was shooting the arrows. From the use of this word here, it seems that he had not seen the dog and had only heard it bark.
s7-fn350: A maharatha is a warrior who can take on 10,000 warriors single-handed. An atiratha can take on 60,000.
s7-fn351: Kunti’s son. In this case, Yudhishthira.
s8-fn2: Drona was the sage Bharadvaja’s son.
s8-fn12: Name for Arjuna. Phalguna and Phalguni are used synonymously.
s8-fn13: The arm guard is called a godha. It was made out of leather and fastened to the left arm, to guard against injury from the string of the bow, since most archers were right-handed. A godha is also an alligator or lizard. The ﬁnger protector is called angulitrana. It was also used to protect against injury from the bowstring. It resembled a thimble and was usually used on the thumb. But it could also be used on other ﬁngers.
s8-fn16: That is, tied to his waist.
s8-fn17: Paka was a demon and Paka’s punisher is Indra. Arjuna was Indra’s son.
s8-fn18: The rainbow is called Indra’s bow.
s8-fn19: Indra. There is a suggestion that is left implicit, but becomes a bit more explicit in the next sentence. Indra was looking down fondly not on the arena in general, but on his son, Arjuna. And the sun especially dispersed the clouds so as to favour his son, Karna.
s8-fn22: Because the sons of kings do not ﬁght with those who are of an inferior lineage.
s8-fn24: With the umbrella, this forms the royal insignia and these whisks were usually made out of yak tails.
s8-fn29: One of the twenty-seven nakshatras, actually consisting of six stars, the Pleiades. The Krittikas reared Kartikeya. Hence, the name.
s8-fn31: Dakshina, the preceptor’s fee that has to be paid at the end of the learning process.
s8-fn35: In case the banishing idea was implemented.
s8-fn47: The word ‘mothers’ is used in a general sense, to indicate all the women who were elderly.
s8-fn53: That is, in a riddle, so that no one else could understand. But Vidura and Yudhishthira were both learned, so Yudhishthira understood what Vidura said. Indeed, the text is such that it is difﬁcult to understand what is being obliquely said, except that a sense of danger is being communicated. But since this was a riddle, that’s probably understandable.
s8-fn61: As will become clear, this was not the house Purochana had built.
s8-fn62: Pritha’s son. In this context, Bhimasena.
s8-fn65: Khanaka. Miner is more accurate as a translation. But in this context, digger seems more appropriate.
s8-fn68: A non-Aryan or barbarian. However, a mleccha was actually someone who spoke in a language other than Sanskrit. There is a problem with the critical edition here. In other editions, Vidura spoke to Yudhishthira in mleccha language, so this reference makes sense. However, in the critical edition, Vidura speaking to Yudhishthira in the mleccha language has been excised and what has been left is Vidura speaking to Yudhishthira in a riddle. So this reference doesn’t quite ﬁt.
s8-fn76: Shukra and shuchi mean the summer season. Speciﬁcally, shukra is the month of Jyeshtha. Shuchi is a general term for the hot season, the months of Jyeshtha and Ashada.
s8-fn77: The word used for large tree is vanaspati, which means lord of the forest.
s8-fn80: Nyagrodha. Can also be translated as banyan tree.
s9-fn2: A very tall and stately tree.
s9-fn5: This is the sister, not named thus far. The avoidance of diacritical marks makes it difﬁcult to differentiate the brother from the sister. The brother is Hidimba, while the sister is Hidimbaa. In the translation, Hidimba will be used for both, since the context will make it clear which one is meant, and Hidimbaa seems forced.
s9-fn6: Kama, the god of love. He has no body because he was burnt to ashes by Shiva.
s9-fn13: Bhima. As a son of Pritha, Bhima is also Partha.
s9-fn22: The implication of this statement is that Hidimba would not survive if she did not get Bhima.
s9-fn25: This probably means that Hidimba made the kind of agreement that Ghatotkacha did, that she would return whenever they needed her.
s9-fn26: Indra. Indra gave Karna a weapon that was later used to kill Ghatotkacha and could therefore not be used against Arjuna, since the weapon could only be used once.
s9-fn28: They hastened when their disguise was threatened and travelled slowly when there was no danger.
s9-fn32: The sons of Pritha, that is, the Pandavas.
s10-fn3: The distress, caused by the terror of a rakshasa.
s10-fn4: The name of a famous cow, in particular the cow which grants all that one desires (kamadhenu).
s10-fn6: The wife, the son or the daughter.
s10-fn7: There is a hell (naraka) named punnama. Those who do not have offspring are destined to go there. It is because a child (son) saves the ancestors from going to punnama that the name putra is given.
s10-fn9: The ancestors. That is, by surviving.
s10-fn10: In this context, relatives means the immediate family.
s10-fn11: This is an argument to explain why the Brahmana did not go and live somewhere else.
s10-fn12: Wishing to learn the secret of the power.
s10-fn14: In the sense of restoring life.
s10-fn16: The other Pandavas had gone out, in search of alms. From the subsequent conversation, Yudhishthira had not gone out either. It was only Arjuna, Nakula and Sahadeva who were absent.
s10-fn20: Ayuta; can also be translated as many.
s11-fn1: Ceremony where the bride chooses her own groom. He told them that the svayamvara was being planned.
s11-fn2: Yajnasena is one of Drupada’s names.
s11-fn8: A brahmachari. Because he had been a brahmachari, he had immense powers.
s11-fn9: Drona. A drona was used to carry water and can also be translated as a vessel, rather than cup.
s11-fn10: The son of Prishata, hence Drupada.
s11-fn12: That is, going away to the forest after giving away all his wealth.
s11-fn16: Chhatravati is another name for Ahichhatra, which was the capital of the kingdom of Panchala.
s11-fn18: As will become evident later, Drupada’s wife accompanied him on his travels.
s11-fn20: The word snataka has many nuances. Here it means a Brahmana who has completed his ﬁrst course of study.
s11-fn24: This inconsistency in the number of cows offered to Yaja exists in the text.
s11-fn26: A qualiﬁcation is needed for this translation. The word used is mithuna, and what seems to be suggested is an act of intercourse between the queen and Yaja. One can also translate this sentence as, ‘The time for the twins has arrived’, meaning the births of Droupadi and Dhrishtadyumna, but this doesn’t seem to be very natural as a translation.
s11-fn28: Dhrishtata means courage and conﬁdence, and dyumna means splendour, glory or lustre.
s11-fn37: Kunapa. This can also mean those who smell like corpses and is therefore also used as a contemptuous term for those who are alive.
s11-fn38: Shatakratu is a performer of 100 sacriﬁces and is one of Indra’s names.
s11-fn39: There was no danger from this revelation of identity, since it wouldn’t have reached the Kouravas.
s11-fn40: One with an adorned chariot; ratha is chariot.
s11-fn41: One whose chariot has been burnt.
s11-fn42: Chakshu means eye. That is the reason the knowledge is called chakshushi.
s11-fn43: The word used in the text is yaviyasa, which means Shudra.
s11-fn44: The word vadava means a mare.
s11-fn45: This is now a reference to the horses the gandharva is giving.
s11-fn46: Bibhatsu is one of Arjuna’s names.
s11-fn47: An explanation is needed about the giving of a weapon. This act of giving doesn’t involve the physical act of giving something and has more to do with teaching the mantras required to release and withdraw a weapon. Therefore, giving a weapon away did not mean that the giver ceased to possess it. What was being given was the knowledge.
s11-fn48: Keep ﬁre burning in the household.
s11-fn51: The next sentence makes it clear that this is the sun-god.
s11-fn53: The one with many rays; a name for the sun.
s11-fn55: Another name for Lakshmi, the goddess of wealth.
s11-fn56: The word used is rambha, which means a plantain tree. Thus, one with thighs like a plantain tree. However, Rambha is also the name of a beautiful apsara and one can also translate this as one with thighs like those of Rambha.
s11-fn58: This can mean different things. It can mean a rainbow, described as Indra’s bow (Shakra is another name for Indra), when the time is right for a rainbow. Alternatively, it can mean a ﬂag erected when the time is right for Indra’s festival.
s11-fn65: Gopati means lord of cows and is another name for the sun.
s11-fn72: Vishvamitra and his sons are known as the Kushikas. Kushika was Vishvamitra’s grandfather. According to some accounts, Kushika was Vishvamitra’s father.
s11-fn73: The six different tastes are katu (pungent), amla (sour), madhur (sweet), lavana (salty), tikta (bitter) and kashaya (astringent).
s11-fn75: In this shloka, the cow’s name is given as Nandi. However, Nandini is more common and subsequent shlokas say Nandini.
s11-fn79: Often used as a term for the Persians.
s11-fn83: Mountainous tribes who lived through hunting.
s11-fn85: Identiﬁed with the inhabitants of Sri Lanka.
s11-fn87: Inhabitants of a country to the north.
s11-fn88: Barbarians, non-Aryans, those who did not speak Sanskrit.
s11-fn90: Nectar, ambrosia, the beverage of the gods.
s11-fn91: In the sense that the king should have yielded the way to a Brahmana.
s11-fn96: Mitrasaha can also be translated as ‘with his friends’. However, no friends have been mentioned. So Mitrasaha is more likely to be one of Kalmashapada’s names.
s11-fn97: Pasha means noose or bond and Vipasha is something that frees one from a bond. Vipasha is identiﬁed with the river Beas.
s11-fn98: Himavati can be the speciﬁc name for a river. Alternatively, it can also be a general term for any river descending from the Himalayas.
s11-fn99: Shatadru is a river with a hundred streams and is identiﬁed as the river Sutlej.
s11-fn100: This is a reference to the six Vedangas.
s11-fn101: To his hermitage. The unblemished one is Vashishtha.
s11-fn105: The word used is sandhya, which can mean dawn, as well as dusk.
s11-fn106: This story is taken up later, and we learn why Kalmashapada requested Vashishtha to impregnate the queen.
s11-fn108: Meaning the king, who had now been freed from the curse of the rakshasa.
s11-fn114: There are fourteen lokas (worlds). Seven are above the ground and seven are below. This is a reference to the seven that are above the ground—bhurloka, bhuvarloka, svarloka, maharloka, janarloka, taparloka and brahmaloka (satyaloka).
s11-fn115: That is, unless something is done about the anger.
s11-fn117: A horse is vadava and a horse’s head is called vadavamukha, mukha meaning face. This submarine ﬁre is thus called vadavamukha. Since anala means ﬁre, it is also called vadavanala.
s11-fn119: Because they had nothing to do with Shakti’s death.
s12-fn1: Victory interpreted as winning Droupadi’s hand.
s12-fn5: Agallochum, a soft, resinous wood with an aromatic smell burnt as perfume.
s12-fn6: That is, those of the lunar dynasty, an epithet for the Panchalas.
s12-fn7: Kandarpa, one of the names of the god of love.
s12-fn14: Halayudha is one of Balarama’s names. Hala means plough, ayudha means weapon. Halayudha is one with the plough as one’s weapon.
s12-fn19: Therefore, they did not notice the Pandavas.
s12-fn20: Pritha is Kunti’s name and only Yudhishthira, Bhima and Arjuna were Kunti’s sons. Nakula and Sahadeva were Madri’s sons.
s12-fn22: The science of archery. In general, the knowledge of arms.
s12-fn23: Varnas; the Shudras are not mentioned here.
s12-fn25: Bhima and Arjuna, who had not left the assembly. Arjuna had only left the arena.
s12-fn28: Usually a name used for Krishna, but here used for Balarama.
s12-fn29: Karaka, more speciﬁcally, water pots made out of coconut shells.
s12-fn32: In the shower of arrows, because they were swiftly shooting arrows at each other.
s12-fn39: Purandara is Indra’s name. Hence, Indra’s weapon.
s12-fn42: It is not very clear who this Brahmana leading the way is, and must be a reference to Dhoumya.
s12-fn48: Meaning, the south. This is a reference to the sage Agastya’s journey to the south.
s12-fn50: The own varna means a Kshatriya. The higher varna means a Brahmana.
s12-fn51: The word used is vama, which means left, as well as lower being. In this context, lower being seems best.
s12-fn52: The word ‘sons’ is used here in the sense of descendants.
s12-fn53: The daityas. Although the word Indra isn’t directly used, the suggestion is clearly that he resembled Indra.
s12-fn55: Yudhishthira, Nakula and Sahadeva, who had returned earlier.
s12-fn60: Tribe, family or race, for want of a better word.
s13-fn4: That is, to ﬁnd out the caste they belonged to.
s13-fn8: The arrangement for the marriage would depend on what caste the Pandavas came from.
s13-fn11: The Pandavas. Drupada assured them that he would help them get back their kingdom.
s13-fn18: Dhrishtadyumna. The text is such that it could also mean Drupada, ruler of Panchala.
s13-fn21: Meaning probably Drupada. Parshata is a family name, used for both Drupada and Dhrishtadyumna.
s13-fn31: Indra, the vajra is Indra’s weapon.
s13-fn34: The king of mountains (giriraja) means high and lofty mountains, but is also a name for the Himalayas.
s13-fn36: The other god. As will become clear, this other god was none other than Shiva or Mahadeva.
s13-fn38: Shiva or Mahadeva; literally the lord of the mountains.
s13-fn39: One of Indra’s names; literally the performer of a hundred sacriﬁces.
s13-fn53: Parshata is one of Drupada’s names.
s13-fn60: That is, Bhima got married on the next day, Arjuna on the next, and so on.
s13-fn62: That is, she regained her virginity after the earlier day’s marriage.
s14-fn10: Son of Radha, that is, Karna.
s14-fn15: Those employed by the Kouravas to sow seeds of dissension and jealousy in the minds of the Pandavas.
s14-fn26: A distant ancestor of both the Kouravas and the Pandavas; the son of Dushyanta and Shakuntala and the origin of the name Bharatavarsha.
s14-fn27: The slayer of Paka, a demon, is Indra.
s14-fn28: A complete army had four components (chaturanga)—elephants, chariots, cavalry and infantry.
s14-fn40: A famous king who performed many sacriﬁces.
s15-fn6: The word used here for Hastinapura is Nagasahrya.
s15-fn14: Weapons that could kill a hundred warriors at a time.
s15-fn15: The Pandavas were also descended from Kuru.
s15-fn22: Variety of tree, another name for the champaka.
s15-fn28: Fragrant tree that buds at the time of rains.
s15-fn32: Tree with red or white ﬂowers.
s15-fn44: Dwaraka or Dwarika, in today’s Gujarat. Sometimes, Dvarvati is used instead of Dvaravati. Literally, the place with gates or doors.
